TextENGNew: "...Before long, that 'something'... came to be called Oyashiro-sama."
"...I've never heard a word of any such rumors!"
"It's a trait of the Japanese temperament—when something is deemed ominous, people seal their lips. ...By not speaking of it, they try to make it as if it never happened."
changeReason: Old translation adds 'workers' as subject not present in Japanese; new correctly uses general 'people'. Also 'taboo' vs 'ominous' (忌み) shifts nuance.

TextENGNew: "...But then it happened. ...A second incident. A third. ...With each new strange event, they grew more frightened of Oyashiro-sama's curse and closed their mouths all the tighter."
"...Management grew troubled by the situation and, it appears, moved to suppress the truth as much as possible."
changeReason: New adds 'it appears' matching the Japanese tentative ようです; old presents the cover-up as definite fact.

TextENGNew: "That can't be...! ...Staff members are dying, and they're covering it up?! That's inconceivable!"
"Not that they were hiding bodies, nothing like that. What I mean is they exercised strict control over information so that it wouldn't reach the ears of anyone uninvolved."
"They didn't want to drag morale down any further. ...But those who know, know. ...Don't they, Arakawa-san?"
"...That... was an accident!"
"...Arakawa... kun...!"
"...Hahahaha! ...Give us a break already! ...Ahahahahaha!"
"...What exactly is funny?"
"I-I mean... you can't honestly expect us not to laugh. ...Hahahaha! ...Come on! In this day and age!"
"Ahahahahaha! Go ahead and laugh!"
"We laughed! We did!"
"We laughed and said what nonsense, something like that couldn't possibly be real! Me too! My colleagues! Everyone! Every one of us!"
changeReason: Old uses 'friends' while new uses 'colleagues' for 仲間, which is more accurate in this workplace context.

TextENGNew: "And then it happened again. The next victim was a man named Kakinuma-san from the Urban Rivers Office."
"Numa-san's was an accident! ...It had nothing to do with any curse!"
"...Were you friends?"
"...N-No. ...He's a colleague I didn't know."
"He was a man who loved drinking and gambling, and always had a laugh ready. ...He was my direct predecessor in this post, and despite the age difference, we went out for drinks often!"
"And yet he'd drink the night away every single evening, even though he had a beautiful wife who looked a full decade younger!"
"...You should go home at a decent hour once in a while, I told him."
changeReason: Old paraphrases as third-person narration; new correctly renders it as direct speech matching the Japanese dialogue format.

TextENGNew: "Listen here, Ara-chan. Don't go getting married, you hear me? The best years are the ones when you're single and can do whatever you please!"
"...He seemed to be wearing down. It had been one gruelling day after another, so I thought it was only natural."
"The last train had long since gone. ...Numa-san was driving me home as he often did. ...And that's when I said to him—Numa-san, you look exhausted."
"...Lately the nights have been rough... I can't seem to get any real sleep."
"Ha! ...Hahahaha! ...Numa-san, come on! ...Don't tell me it's showing up at your place too? The you-know-what."
"...............You haven't felt anything yourself, Ara-chan? ...At night, say?"
"Feel something at night? ...Hahaha! That sounds a bit risqué!"
"...There's definitely something there. ......Something's there."
changeReason: Old uses 'they're there' (plural entities) while new uses 'something there' (singular/ambiguous); the Japanese "いる" is ambiguous about number, so this changes reader interpretation.

TextENGNew: "...When I can't stand it I turn the light on. ...But all that's there is my wife and the kid. Of course, right? ...........And yet, there's still something there."
"...Numa-san, please... ...Something's there, something's there—what in the world is supposed to be there?"
"...Kondou-san, you know. ...He was saying the same sort of thing about a month before... wasn't he..."
"Kondou-san, well, he was just—you know! ...He had that kind of... tendency to begin with..."
changeReason: Old implies the situations were similar; new implies Kondou-san had a pre-existing tendency (そういう所がありました), shifting the characterization of Kondou.

TextENGNew: "...Maybe the same one that's been coming to my place... ...was there at Kondou-san's place too."
"...Let's drop it. The Kondou-san business!"
"...Yeah, I know. ...But the thing Kondou-san said was there... that thing is probably him."
"...He's definitely there. ...With every passing day, I feel his presence more and more strongly."
"Can we please stop this. ...This isn't funny anymore!"
"...Yeah! ...Let's stop. ...But you know, day by day the feeling gets stronger. I'm not kidding."
"...Strong enough that I almost feel if I were to open my eyes... I'd find him standing right there."
The lights go out. The stage is plunged into darkness.
"...You know what? ...I think I'll be meeting him soon. ...Oyashiro-sama."
"...That very night, in the small hours. ...He drove into an oncoming dump truck and was killed."
"It was an accident! ...However close it followed that conversation! ...It was an accident!"
"...But I couldn't possibly tell his wife about that conversation! ...A story that absurd—how could anyone say that to her!"
"At first it was thought he had fallen asleep at the wheel. But that was not the case. ...Kakinuma-san's body was found with the throat clawed raw."
"Both hands were drenched in blood, and some of the nails had been torn from his fingers."
"...I'm having some trouble following all this..."
"...In other words, in the moments before he died, for some reason he began clawing at his own throat. ...With such terrible force that it ripped his nails right off."
"How do you know about that?! ...That detail, it..."
changeReason: Old implies suspicion about Yoshimura's identity ('Could you be...'); new focuses on the restricted detail ('That detail, it...'), matching the Japanese その事は which refers to the confidential information.

TextENGNew: "I dug into it. ...I am a writer, after all, however modest."
changeReason: Old translates ライター as 'reporter' while new uses 'writer'; 端くれ (lit. 'merely at the edge of') is rendered as 'something of a' vs 'however modest', with the new being more precise.

TextENGNew: "...But why would you go to those lengths..."
changeReason: Old translates どうして as 'how' (method) while new correctly translates it as 'why' (motivation), changing the nature of the question.

TextENGNew: "...He met him. ...Oyashiro-sama."
Distant thunder.@k@rThe sound of rain.@k@rA heavy silence settles over them.
"Are you aware of what happened to Mori-san and Katou-san?"
"I don't! I genuinely don't know about either of them! ...Katou-san I only heard about in passing... I don't know the details!"
"Katou-san threw himself in front of a train and was killed."
"...Station employees witnessed him acting strangely in the moments before he jumped, but the details remain unclear."
"I don't know, I don't know, I don't know!"
"What about Mori-san? That happened at the office, didn't it? You should have been at work that day."
"At the office?! ...I knew nothing about that! What happened?!"
"...I don't know! I'd gone out for lunch! ...When I came back there was all this commotion... and it was already over!"
"By the time I got back it was all done with! ...So I don't know! I don't know anything!"
"This is the first I've heard of it! ...If something like that happened at the office, how could I not have known?!"

"...By that point Mori-san was the fourth, after all. It seems that management had already made arrangements with senior police regarding 'special circumstances,' so even the scene investigation was conducted entirely in secret."
"...Do you recall? ...There was a day when there was a commotion over an accident during gas valve replacement work."
"...Now that you mention it... ...They said a contractor and the staff member present were injured and taken away... ...I do seem to remember a day of uproar like that..."
"That was a cover story, naturally. The truth is a very different matter."
"...A cover story?"
changeReason: Old has generic question 'What do you mean?' while new correctly echoes カバーストーリー？ as 'A cover story?' matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: "...A fabricated account. ...A false story designed to hide the truth."
changeReason: Old implies 'they used their authority' which is not in the Japanese; new accurately renders the definition of cover story from the JPN without adding extra meaning.

TextENGNew: "...Those involved kept their silence with remarkable determination, so I lack full details, but the following is certain."
"...Near the end of the lunch break, Mori-san, who had been sleeping at his desk, suddenly bolted upright and attacked the colleague beside him with an awl."
"He then attacked three more staff members... and before he could be restrained, he drove the awl into his own throat and died. It was all over in the space of a minute or two."
"That's why I told you—I was out for lunch! I don't know anything!"
The stage darkens.@k@rA light falls on Arakawa.@k@rHe is reliving the moment when Mori backed him into a corner, awl in hand.
"Please, Mori-san, stop! There's nothing there! No one's there!"
"There's nothing there! There isn't anything! Please calm down!"
"It's a folk tale! ...There's nothing there! Oyashiro-sama doesn't existttttt!"
"...Bathroom. ...I'm sorry. ...Please excuse me again."
Arakawa leaves the stage, his face strained.@k@r...The sound of rain returns.
"...I had no idea. ...That things like that had..."
"The true nature of Oyashiro-sama's curse remains unknown. ...But it undeniably exists. And the key to unravelling that mystery must lie here—in Hinamizawa."
"...That is why I have come. To investigate that something."
"...You'll die."
"Pardon?"
"...You will die too. ...The very act of seeking out Oyashiro-sama's true nature is itself a desecration."
"...If that is why you have come to Hinamizawa... then you will die."
A leaden silence. Yoshimura says nothing.
"...The bus is certainly taking its time. ...Shouldn't it have come by now?"
"...Haha... running late, probably. ...Given the rain."
The stage slowly goes dark, accompanied by the sound of rain.@k@rA moment of silence.@k@rThe stage remains pitch black. A voice crackles over a radio.
changeReason: Old says rain sound 'fades out' which isn't in the Japanese; new correctly describes the stage darkening accompanied by rain, matching 雨の音と共に舞台、徐々に暗転.

TextENGNew: "...Reconnaissance unit, reporting. ...There are outsiders among the group."
"...That was not anticipated. ...Do we abort?"
